question: Will City Hall be open on Monday April 20th?
expected_answer: No, City Hall and all City buildings (excluding public safety) will be closed on Monday, April 20, 2026 for Patriots' Day observance. They will reopen on Tuesday, April 21 during normal business hours.
source_title: Patriots' Day Observance
actual_answer:
No, City Hall will be closed on Monday, April 20th in observance of Patriots' Day. City buildings will reopen during normal business hours on Tuesday, April 21st.

However, you can still access many services online during the closure, including bill payments at https://www.beverlyma.gov/556/Online-Payment. Public safety buildings like the police station will remain open.

For more details, see the [Patriots' Day Observance announcement](https://www.beverlyma.gov/m/newsflash/Home/Detail/367).

question: What is the Beverly Housing Rehabilitation Program and when will the city request funding for it?
expected_answer: The Beverly Housing Rehabilitation Program (BHRP) is a program that will be funded through Massachusetts Community Development Block Grant (CDBG) Program Community Development Fund 2025 funds. The City of Beverly, through the Department of Planning & Development, will request the release of these funds on or about May 1, 2026.
source_title: Notice of Intent to Request Release of Funds
actual_answer:
# Beverly Housing Rehabilitation Program

The **Beverly Housing Rehabilitation Program (BHRP)** is a city program that uses Massachusetts Community Development Block Grant (CDBG) Program funds to help with housing rehabilitation projects in Beverly.

## Funding Request Timeline

The City of Beverly, through the Department of Planning & Development, will request the release of these state funds **on or about May 1, 2026**. The program will kick off sometime after the funds are released.

## More Information

For full details about the program, visit: **www.beverlyma.gov/CDBG**

You can also view the Environmental Review Record (ERR) documenting the environmental determinations for this project at the Beverly Planning & Development Department, 191 Cabot Street during business hours.

**Note:** The public comment period for this program ended on April 25, 2026.

For official announcements, see: https://www.beverlyma.gov/m/newsflash/Home/Detail/366

question: Will I have to pay for the new water meter installation?
expected_answer: No, the meter and reading device are supplied and installed at no cost to the water customer.
source_title: Info For City Wide Water Meter Change-Out Program
actual_answer:
No, you will not have to pay for the new water meter installation. According to the City of Beverly's Water Meter Changeout Program announcement, **the meter and reading device are supplied and installed at no cost to the water customer**.

The installation is mandatory and is being performed by the City's contractor, Hydro Utilities. You will receive an official letter from Hydro Utilities with instructions and program details when the contractor is working in your area.

For more details, see the [Water Meter Changeout Program announcement](https://www.beverlyma.gov/m/newsflash/Home/Detail/362).

actual_answer:
Yes, Beverly updated its immigration policy for police. In February 2026, Mayor Mike Cahill, in consultation with Police Chief LeLacheur, City Council President Flowers, and School Committee President Visnick, issued a joint proclamation reaffirming the community's values and commitment to the safety of all Beverly residents, including immigrant residents.

**Key update:** Chief LeLacheur updated the **Beverly Police Department Immigration Status and Detainer Policy** (the previous version was from 2017).

You can find more details in the joint proclamation and the updated policy documents:
- [Joint Proclamation: Immigration and the Values of the City of Beverly](https://www.beverlyma.gov/m/newsflash/Home/Detail/363)
- [Mayoral Declaration - Immigration 2.9.2026](https://www.beverlyma.gov/m/newsflash/Home/Detail/363)
- [Immigration Proclamation 2.9.2026](https://www.beverlyma.gov/m/newsflash/Home/Detail/363)
- [Beverly Police Department - Immigration Status and Detainer Policy](https://www.beverlyma.gov/m/newsflash/Home/Detail/363)

question: Why is there a new Public Works Administration unit in the FY24 budget?
expected_answer: It's a newly restructured unit that consolidates central support administrators and staff from the Highway Division and Engineering Department into a single unit for better coordination across all Public Services divisions. There is no change to the total number of positions.
source_title: Beverly MA FY2024 Proposed Operating Budget — Page 205
actual_answer:
# Public Works Administration Unit Creation (FY24)

According to the FY2024 budget, the Public Works Administration unit was newly restructured in FY24 for **organizational efficiency reasons**:

- It consolidates various positions from the **Highway Division and the Engineering Department** into a single unit
- There is **no overall FTE (full-time equivalent) impact** — no new positions were created
- The restructuring places **central support administrators and staff into one unit** rather than spreading them across multiple divisions
- This unified structure allows the unit to **administer and coordinate among all other Department of Public Services divisions** going forward

In other words, it's a reorganization designed to centralize administrative functions and improve coordination across Public Services divisions.

**Sources:** 
- [FY2024 Budget - Page 205](https://www.beverlymass.gov/)
- [FY2025 Budget - Page 206](https://www.beverlymass.gov/)

question: What are the main goals for Beverly Public Schools in FY2026?
expected_answer: The three core goals are: fostering a sense of belonging for every student and staff member, empowering educators through meaningful professional growth opportunities, and upholding a commitment to innovative practices and the highest educational standards.
source_title: Beverly MA FY2026 Proposed Operating Budget — Page 6
actual_answer:
Based on the FY2026 Beverly Public Schools budget, the district has three main strategic goals:

1. **Fostering Belonging** — Foster an enhanced culture of respect, inclusivity, and collaboration, where all district stakeholders feel valued and empowered to make positive changes in their schools and communities.

2. **Empowering Educators** — Support all staff to develop a deep understanding of effective high-impact instructional strategies and how to use them to transform student engagement and performance.

3. **Driving Innovative Practices** — Beverly Public Schools will implement targeted, innovative, learner-centered experiences that allow students to apply their knowledge to authentic situations, with a focus on civics understanding to promote active and informed citizenship and to empower students to make positive contributions toward addressing climate change.

These goals align with the district's mission to maximize academic achievement and personal growth, enabling all students to compete in the global economy.

question: Why did the FY2024 budget increase so much compared to last year?
expected_answer: The budget increased by $6,944,500 primarily due to non-discretionary spending requirements and modest revenue increases. The city addressed the initial imbalance by refining requested funding, pulling some capital investments into FY2023, and relying on steady revenue streams to cover cost increases.
source_title: Beverly MA FY2024 Proposed Operating Budget — Page 21
actual_answer:
According to the FY2024 Proposed Operating Budget, the budget increased by **$6,944,500** compared to FY2023. The main reasons for this significant increase are:

1. **Payroll and Benefits**: As Beverly is a service delivery organization, the vast majority of costs relate to personnel and benefits. All city employee contracts are current with prescribed salary adjustments scheduled for FY2024, requiring additional adjustment for most payroll-related lines compared to FY2023.

2. **Non-Discretionary Spending**: The budget document notes that "non-discretionary spending requirements coupled with modest revenue increases created a landscape that was significantly out of balance initially."

3. **Specific Cost Areas**: While the document mentions there are "increasing and decreasing costs in almost every area of the budget," it highlights that certain areas had notable cost drivers, though the specific details are cut off in the available excerpt.

The city addressed these challenges through budget refinement, pulling some capital investments into FY2023 to avoid those expenses in FY2024, and relying on steady revenue streams to cover the cost increases.

For more detailed information about specific departmental increases, you may want to review the full FY2024 budget document at the provided URL.

question: How much is the General Fund budget increasing in FY2025?
expected_answer: The General Fund budget is proposed to increase by 5.38% over the FY2024 budget.
source_title: Beverly MA FY2025 Proposed Operating Budget — Page 22
actual_answer:
Based on the Beverly MA FY2025 Proposed Operating Budget, the **General Fund budget is increasing by 5.4%**, from $157,526,500 in FY2024 to $166,004,910 in FY2025.

This represents an increase of **$8,478,410** in total budgeted expenditures.

The largest increases by expense type are:
- **Purchase of Services: +11.7%** ($9.6M to $10.8M)
- **Other Charges/Expenditures: +8.2%** ($75.4M to $81.6M)
- **Supplies: +5.9%** ($2.7M to $2.8M)

Conversely, **Debt Service is decreasing by 7.6%** and **Capital Outlay is decreasing by 6.5%**.

Source: [Beverly MA FY2025 Proposed Operating Budget — Page 35](https://www.ci.beverly.ma.us/)

question: Why does Beverly's budget use different accounting methods than regular GAAP accounting?
expected_answer: Beverly uses budgetary basis accounting in accordance with Massachusetts state law and Department of Revenue (DOR) accounting practices. This method is required for the city's annual budget and property tax certification process, even though it departs from generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) in several ways.
source_title: Beverly MA FY2025 Proposed Operating Budget — Page 17
actual_answer:
According to Beverly's budget documents, the City uses the "budgetary basis" method of accounting rather than GAAP because it is **required by Massachusetts State law**. 

Specifically, the Massachusetts Department of Revenue (DOR) establishes accounting practices that Beverly must follow for its annual budget and property tax certification process. This budgetary basis method is mandated under state law to ensure that:

1. All cities and towns have balanced budgets
2. Tax levies stay within the limits set by Proposition 2½

The budgetary basis method differs from GAAP in several key ways:
- Property taxes are recorded when levied (rather than when accrued)
- Encumbrances and continuing appropriations are treated as expenditures
- Certain activities are presented as components of the general fund (rather than separate funds)
- Prior year deficits and surpluses are handled differently

This standardized approach ensures statewide consistency and fiscal responsibility across Massachusetts municipalities.

*Source: Beverly MA FY2025 Proposed Operating Budget, Page 17*

question: How much is the Fire Department budget increasing for FY2024?
expected_answer: The Fire Department budget is increasing by $327,910, which represents a 4.13% increase compared to the prior year.
source_title: Beverly MA FY2024 Proposed Operating Budget — Page 171
actual_answer:
According to the FY2024 Proposed Operating Budget, the Fire Department budget is increasing by **$327,910, or 4.13%** compared to the FY2023 budget.

This increase is driven by:
1. **Collective bargaining obligations** - a 2.75% salary adjustment for employees
2. **Overtime (shift coverage) adjustments** - to consistently staff 4 trucks while accounting for injuries, military deployments, and scheduled time off

The FY2024 budgeted amount is **$8,264,162**.

question: How far ahead does Beverly's capital plan look?
expected_answer: The plan covers a five-year period as required by the Beverly City Charter, but also includes projects that may need attention within the next ten years to support long-term financial and service planning.
source_title: Beverly MA FY2024 Proposed Operating Budget — Page 302
actual_answer:
According to Beverly's Capital Plan, the city looks ahead **ten years**.

The Beverly City Charter requires an annual submission of a capital improvement plan that includes a list of all capital improvements proposed over the next five years. However, as stated in both the FY2024 and FY2025 budget documents, the plan intentionally extends beyond that minimum requirement to include "projects that may require attention within the next ten years to help aid in long term financial and service planning."

This longer planning horizon helps the city better prepare for future capital needs and make informed decisions about priorities and affordability.

question: How much money is being transferred for road and sidewalk work in FY25?
expected_answer: The proposed budget includes $1.25M transfer to a paving account for road and sidewalk work. This will be combined with other resources like State Chapter 90 funds and grant funding.
source_title: Beverly MA FY2025 Proposed Operating Budget — Page 289
actual_answer:
Based on the FY2025 budget data, **$1.25 million** is being transferred from the General Fund to a paving account for road and sidewalk work in FY25.

However, it's important to note that this $1.25M represents only part of the total road and sidewalk funding. According to the Mayor's budget message, Beverly is investing **over $4.35 million total for roads and sidewalks during FY2025**, which combines:
- The $1.25M proposed budget line item
- Free cash transfers (the budget mentions a request to transfer $2M of the City's Free Cash balance)
- State Chapter 90 funds
- Grant funding for road and sidewalk work

Additionally, the FY25 budget includes a **$45,000 transfer to a landfill monitoring fund** in this section.

Sources: Beverly MA FY2025 Proposed Operating Budget, Pages 5, 23, and 289
